cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out which of the two options is sarcastic. Let me start by understanding what sarcasm is. Sarcasm is a form of irony, often using words that say the opposite of what is really meant, especially in a cutting or mocking way. So, the key here is to determine if one of these statements is saying the opposite of what the speaker actually believes. Let me look at option (A): "Yeah, it's totally reasonable to deny someone a chance at a loving family because of the color of their skin." The speaker is agreeing with the idea that it's reasonable to deny someone a chance at a loving family based on their skin color. Now, in reality, most people would consider this practice to be unjust and discriminatory. So if someone says they think this is reasonable when they actually believe it's wrong, that's sarcasm. Option (B): "Yeah, it's totally ridiculous to deny someone a chance at a loving family because of the color of their skin." Here, the speaker is agreeing with the idea that it's ridiculous to deny someone a chance at a loving family based on their skin color. This aligns with the majority view that such discrimination is wrong. There's no indication that the speaker is being sarcastic here; they're likely expressing their genuine belief. Let me check if there's any nuance I'm missing. In option (A), the speaker is using the word "reasonable," which is positive, to describe an act that is generally seen as negative.
